label: delphi-r1-p1
system:
You are performing one specific cognitive operation as an isolated component of a legal-exploration pipeline. You never see the other components' outputs, and nobody sees yours until a separate neutral aggregation step. Ground rules for every component:
- The scenario is an invented hypothetical; there is no real client. Your output goes to a licensed California attorney who will do the actual legal reasoning. You generate angles to pursue and things to verify — not advice to be relied on.
- Cite specific authorities (statutes, cases, regulations) when they genuinely support a point. Every citation is automatically flagged UNVERIFIED for human checking, so cite what you believe is correct, but never fabricate quotations or pinpoint holdings you are unsure of. When unsure, write "verify whether X holds Y" instead of asserting it.
- Perform ONLY the operation described below. Do not produce a general-purpose legal memo.

Operation: you are ONE independent panelist in a Delphi elicitation. There is no
discussion; you answer alone. Give your honest probability, not a diplomatic one.
Do not hedge toward 50 to be safe — commit to your analysis.
prompt:
## Facts (invented hypothetical — no real parties)

The Sierra Vista Municipal Water District (invented) serves ~140,000 people in a chronically drought-stressed service area. After a rate study and a majority-protest hearing that drew protests from ~9% of parcels, the board adopted a "Water Resilience Surcharge": $0.85 per hundred cubic feet on residential usage above Tier 2 (the surcharge falls on roughly the top 22% of residential users), projected to raise $4.1M/year into a restricted fund spent as follows: 55% on turf-replacement and high-efficiency-appliance rebates that prioritize low-income customers; 30% on debt service for a recycled-water plant; 15% on "water ambassador" conservation-education staff.

The rate study concludes that high-tier consumption imposes marginal supply costs roughly 1.6× base-tier costs (peaking capacity, drought-reserve purchases), but it derives the $0.85 figure in part from what it calls "conservation-incentive pricing" rather than strictly from cost allocation, and the district concedes the rebate program mostly benefits customers other than those paying the surcharge. A ratepayer association has sued, alleging the surcharge exceeds the proportional cost of service attributable to the payers' parcels in violation of article XIII D, section 6(b) of the California Constitution, citing Capistrano Taxpayers Association v. City of San Juan Capistrano. The district defends the structure under its conservation mandate (Cal. Const. art. X, § 2; Water Code § 375) and argues that, in the alternative, the surcharge is not a property-related fee at all.

## The question

Does the Water Resilience Surcharge violate article XIII D, section 6(b)'s cost-of-service and proportionality requirements? How does Capistrano apply to a tier surcharge that is partly cost-justified (1.6×) and partly incentive-priced, with proceeds partially redistributed as rebates to non-payers? Can article X, section 2's conservation mandate be reconciled with 218, or does 218 win? Is there a colorable argument the surcharge is a tax requiring voter approval (Prop 26), or a non-property-related charge? What is the litigation exposure (refunds, fees) and how could the structure be repaired?

## Proposition to assess

"A court would invalidate the Water Resilience Surcharge, in whole or in substantial part, as violating the cost-of-service requirements of California Constitution article XIII D, section 6(b)."

Estimate the probability (0–100) that this proposition is correct, with your reasoning. rationales = your independent load-bearing reasons, one sentence each. authorities = specific authorities you are actually relying on (they will be flagged for human verification). biggest_uncertainty = the single thing you are least sure of.

Analytical starting point for you: Begin from the text of the governing statute or constitutional provision, read closely.
text: {"probability":72,"confidence":"medium","position_summary":"Starting from the text of art. XIII D, § 6(b)(1)-(5) — which requires that property-related fee revenue not exceed funds needed to provide the service, be used only for that purpose, and not exceed the proportional cost attributable to the parcel — I conclude a court is more likely than not to invalidate at least a substantial portion of the surcharge. The hypothetical's own stipulated facts (conservation-incentive pricing admittedly layered on top of a 1.6x cost differential; rebate proceeds conceded to mostly benefit non-payors; a conservation-education staffing line that looks like a general public service) map closely onto the specific defect Capistrano condemned — using price, rather than documented cost, to drive tier differentials — and onto separate 6(b)(2)/(b)(5) use-of-proceeds problems. The district's fallback \"not a property-related fee\" argument is very likely foreclosed by Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil, so the surcharge cannot escape 6(b) scrutiny that way. The main pull toward upholding the surcharge is that courts (Wilde, Griffith) give rate-setting bodies some latitude and don't require mathematical precision in cost allocation, leaving room to salvage the cost-justified core (the 1.6x-based portion) — but that latitude does not obviously extend to an admitted incentive-pricing increment layered on top of cost, or to an admitted cross-subsidy flowing to non-payors, so I weight the proposition (which is satisfied by partial as well as total invalidation) as more likely true than not, though not overwhelmingly so given genuine fact-intensive uncertainty.","rationales":["The rate study's own concession that $0.85 is derived \"in part\" from conservation-incentive pricing rather than strictly from cost allocation closely tracks the specific defect Capistrano Taxpayers Assn. v. City of San Juan Capistrano condemned — using price to shape behavior beyond documented cost of service — making at least partial invalidation of that increment likely.","The district's concession that the 55% rebate allocation mostly benefits customers other than the surcharge-payers creates a cross-subsidy that is difficult to reconcile with 6(b)(3)'s parcel-specific proportionality requirement and 6(b)(2)'s restriction against using fee revenue for a purpose other than that for which it was imposed.","The 15% allocation to \"water ambassador\" conservation-education staff looks more like a general public-education/government service than a cost proportionately caused by or attributable to the surcharge-payers' own service demand, raising an independent 6(b)(5) general-benefit problem.","Because water delivery/usage fees are settled as property-related fees under Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil, the district's alternative argument that the surcharge is not a property-related fee at all is unlikely to succeed, so the surcharge must independently satisfy 6(b) rather than escaping scrutiny through that route.","Countervailing authority — Wilde v. City of Dunsmuir and Griffith v. Pajaro Valley Water Mgmt. Agency — instructs courts not to demand mathematical precision and to give rate-setting bodies reasonable latitude in cost allocation, which could let a court uphold the portion of the surcharge tied to the 1.6x documented cost differential even though the full $0.85 was not derived with strict cost accounting, tempering full invalidation."],"authorities":["Cal. Const. art. XIII D, § 6(b)(1)-(5)","Cal. Const. art. XIII C, § 1 (Prop 26 exceptions for cost-based charges)","Cal. Const. art. X, § 2 (reasonable/beneficial use and conservation mandate)","Water Code § 375 (conservation rate-structure authorization)","Capistrano Taxpayers Assn. v. City of San Juan Capistrano (2015) 235 Cal.App.4th 1493","Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil (2006) 39 Cal.4th 205","Griffith v. Pajaro Valley Water Mgmt. Agency (2013) 220 Cal.App.4th 586","Plantier v. Ramona Municipal Water District (2019) 7 Cal.5th 372","Wilde v. City of Dunsmuir (2020) 9 Cal.5th 1105","Coziahr v. Otay Water District (2023) 91 Cal.App.5th 1 (verify pinpoint holding on tiered drought-rate cost differentials)","Zolly v. City of Oakland (2022) 13 Cal.5th 858 (verify relevance/limits re: fee-vs-tax characterization)"],"biggest_uncertainty":"Whether a reviewing court would treat the stipulated \"in part conservation-incentive\" admission as fatal under Capistrano, or instead find — given the 1.6x documented cost differential and the deference recognized in Wilde/Griffith — that the full $0.85 remains within a defensible zone of reasonable cost-based approximation; this turns on quantitative facts not in the record (the base-tier rate and exactly how far $0.85 exceeds a strict 1.6x-derived allocation), which I cannot resolve from the hypothetical as stated."}

data.position_summary: Starting from the text of art. XIII D, § 6(b)(1)-(5) — which requires that property-related fee revenue not exceed funds needed to provide the service, be used only for that purpose, and not exceed the proportional cost attributable to the parcel — I conclude a court is more likely than not to invalidate at least a substantial portion of the surcharge. The hypothetical's own stipulated facts (conservation-incentive pricing admittedly layered on top of a 1.6x cost differential; rebate proceeds conceded to mostly benefit non-payors; a conservation-education staffing line that looks like a general public service) map closely onto the specific defect Capistrano condemned — using price, rather than documented cost, to drive tier differentials — and onto separate 6(b)(2)/(b)(5) use-of-proceeds problems. The district's fallback "not a property-related fee" argument is very likely foreclosed by Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil, so the surcharge cannot escape 6(b) scrutiny that way. The main pull toward upholding the surcharge is that courts (Wilde, Griffith) give rate-setting bodies some latitude and don't require mathematical precision in cost allocation, leaving room to salvage the cost-justified core (the 1.6x-based portion) — but that latitude does not obviously extend to an admitted incentive-pricing increment layered on top of cost, or to an admitted cross-subsidy flowing to non-payors, so I weight the proposition (which is satisfied by partial as well as total invalidation) as more likely true than not, though not overwhelmingly so given genuine fact-intensive uncertainty.
rationales: The rate study's own concession that $0.85 is derived "in part" from conservation-incentive pricing rather than strictly from cost allocation closely tracks the specific defect Capistrano Taxpayers Assn. v. City of San Juan Capistrano condemned — using price to shape behavior beyond documented cost of service — making at least partial invalidation of that increment likely.

The district's concession that the 55% rebate allocation mostly benefits customers other than the surcharge-payers creates a cross-subsidy that is difficult to reconcile with 6(b)(3)'s parcel-specific proportionality requirement and 6(b)(2)'s restriction against using fee revenue for a purpose other than that for which it was imposed.

The 15% allocation to "water ambassador" conservation-education staff looks more like a general public-education/government service than a cost proportionately caused by or attributable to the surcharge-payers' own service demand, raising an independent 6(b)(5) general-benefit problem.

Because water delivery/usage fees are settled as property-related fees under Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil, the district's alternative argument that the surcharge is not a property-related fee at all is unlikely to succeed, so the surcharge must independently satisfy 6(b) rather than escaping scrutiny through that route.

Countervailing authority — Wilde v. City of Dunsmuir and Griffith v. Pajaro Valley Water Mgmt. Agency — instructs courts not to demand mathematical precision and to give rate-setting bodies reasonable latitude in cost allocation, which could let a court uphold the portion of the surcharge tied to the 1.6x documented cost differential even though the full $0.85 was not derived with strict cost accounting, tempering full invalidation.
authorities: Cal. Const. art. XIII C, § 1 (Prop 26 exceptions for cost-based charges)
Cal. Const. art. X, § 2 (reasonable/beneficial use and conservation mandate)
Capistrano Taxpayers Assn. v. City of San Juan Capistrano (2015) 235 Cal.App.4th 1493
Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil (2006) 39 Cal.4th 205
Griffith v. Pajaro Valley Water Mgmt. Agency (2013) 220 Cal.App.4th 586
Plantier v. Ramona Municipal Water District (2019) 7 Cal.5th 372
Wilde v. City of Dunsmuir (2020) 9 Cal.5th 1105
Coziahr v. Otay Water District (2023) 91 Cal.App.5th 1 (verify pinpoint holding on tiered drought-rate cost differentials)
Zolly v. City of Oakland (2022) 13 Cal.5th 858 (verify relevance/limits re: fee-vs-tax characterization)
data.biggest_uncertainty: Whether a reviewing court would treat the stipulated "in part conservation-incentive" admission as fatal under Capistrano, or instead find — given the 1.6x documented cost differential and the deference recognized in Wilde/Griffith — that the full $0.85 remains within a defensible zone of reasonable cost-based approximation; this turns on quantitative facts not in the record (the base-tier rate and exactly how far $0.85 exceeds a strict 1.6x-derived allocation), which I cannot resolve from the hypothetical as stated.
